text: only twenty minutes before. The air was thick with dust again, the floor hidden beneath the detritus, and electric wiring and light fixtures hung from the mangled ceiling . Gaining his feet, Ronin crossed the rubble, suspension system straining to keep him upright. The synth with the AMR was pulling himself out from beneath the debris when Ronin found him. He handed the heavy rifle to Ronin without question . Shouldering the AMR, Ronin raised the barrel and took aim at Comp. To his right, rifles roared — the soldiers’, by their sound — and bullets from outside whizzed through the lobby, embedding themselves in the wall . Comp twisted and bent, smacking around chunks rubble, seeking something to break. When the bot’s torso was exposed, Ronin squeezed the trigger. The rifle bucked against his shoulder. He held the barrel level and fired again . The first shot caught Comp in the middle, and its torso ground to a halt. The second bullet punched through the bouncer’s casing and hit its power cell. Electricity arced from its eyes and joints, and flames licked out of the holes in its casing for half a second before the bot stilled, legs locking while its torso and arms sagged . Ronin rose and turned his body fully toward the front door. Maul and several bots stood strong, advancing in a walking line as they fired on the retreating gearheads. More rounds rained from the floors above, taking down two of the fleeing bots before they made it more than a few yards into the grass circle . The fight had turned. The enemy had broken. They were no less dangerous, but their will to fight was, for now , gone . He turned the AMR in his hands. The weapon was familiar, though he had never come across a functioning one in all his time in the Dust. It had a five-round ammunition capacity. Drawing the bolt back partway, he confirmed that a single round remained . So long as Maul had no objections, Ronin planned to use it on Warlord . He crossed the rubble to reach the Sergeant. At least six gearheads lay, deactivated, in and around the doorway. Four of the soldiers were unmoving . “We need to finish this,” Ronin said. Tucking the AMR under his arm, he swapped in the last full magazine for the assault rifle . Maul nodded, and the flap of synthetic skin hanging from his cheek bobbed. He reached up, took hold of it, and tore it off. His jaw-plates shifted subtly. “Yeah. I think that’s a good idea .” Ronin and Maul turned to face the others; several more soldiers had come down from the upper levels. Some bore signs of damaged synthetic skin and casings, others wore sheens of sweat and blood . “Are you ready to show them what an attack looks like?” Maul asked . The soldiers, bot and human alike, offered a wordless, affirmative shout in response . It had only been eighty-two days since Ronin met Lara, since his existence was irrevocably altered, but it felt like an eternity had passed. Now, this part of it would be done. Warlord would be no more . Ronin charged out of the building, leaping over the shattered remnants of the doors, walls, and ceiling. Guns roared before and behind. His optics took in the chaos ahead, and despite the speed of his processors, he didn’t immediately understand what he was looking at . The retreating gearheads — the ones that weren’t damaged beyond function on the grass and pavement — had regrouped with their remaining comrades, and were battling another group that had come from the south. The mob was a mix of humans and bots, of Cheyenne townsfolk and soldiers from the base. Some fought with firearms, but most wielded pipes and prybars and all manner of improvised weaponry . Cooper and a group of soldiers advanced on the shrinking cluster of gearheads. Warlord stood in the center of the enemy group, brows low over the bridge of his nose and lips pulled back in a snarl. The gearheads fired into the crowd, heads turning rapidly to track their many targets, but their leader’s attention was fixed. Ronin followed Warlord’s line of sight . Ronin saw a red braid, and then the soldier in front of Lara — Chester — moved to line up a shot, revealing her completely. She held a large pistol with both hands. It jumped when she pulled the trigger, but she calmly brought it back down and aimed again . Warlord stared directly at her, wearing his hatred plainly upon his face. Though she was positioned at the rear of the mob, one of the most distant targets, he directed his gearheads. As one, they shifted their focus, swinging their weapons toward Lara . If Ronin had a heart, it would have stopped in that moment. He lined up his rifle as the gearheads opened fire. He squeezed the trigger, sending armor piercing rounds into two of the bots’ heads . On his periphery, he saw Chester turn and sweep Lara into his arms, using his back as a shield. Lara cried out; Ronin’s audio receptors isolated the sound from the cacophony. Distress? Terror? Pain? There was no guarantee that Chester would stop all the bullets . Chester’s clothes tore, his skin broke, his body shook with the force. He sagged forward over Lara, catching himself by jabbing the barrel of his rifle into the ground as a support . “Lara!” Ronin shouted . He poured more speed into his charge, jumping directly at the remaining gearheads. Two swung around and fired; he registered new damage to his torso casing, but didn’t waste the computing power to assess it. The only priority was to stop them from shooting any more . His momentum knocked them to the ground. Despite the groans of protest from his hip, he swung himself up onto a knee and
